context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 1


Problem 1.1 (20 pts)

For the mass and spring discussed during the lecture (Howard Georgi Eq. (1.1)–(1.8)), suppose that the system is hung vertically in the earth's gravitational field, with the top of the spring held fixed. Show that the frequency for vertical oscillations is given by Eq. (1.5). Explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.


Problem 1.2 (20 pts)

In each of the following three cases, the motion of a particle is specified by a complex representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find a real representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    η(t) = c cos ωt + d sin ωt
where c and d are real, time-independent quantities. A, B, C, and τ are real quantities. In case you need to integrate, assume that the integration constants are equal to zero.

(a) X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2))
(b) Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6))
(c) Ẍ(t) = C·(1/(1 + iωτ))·e^(−iωt)

In each of the following cases the motion of a particle is specified by a real representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find the complex representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    Q(t) = Q·e^(−i(ωt + φ))
where Q and φ are real, time-independent quantities. α, β, γ, and δ are real and positive quantities.

(d) x(t) = α cos ωt − β sin ωt
(e) ẋ(t) = −γ sin(ωt + π/3)
(f) ẍ(t) = δ cos(ωt + π/6)


Problem 1.3 (20 pts)

A block of mass M slides without friction between two springs of spring constants K and 2K as shown in Figure 1. The system is constrained to move only along the axis of the springs.

[Figure 1: Oscillating mass — a block M between a left spring (constant K) fixed to a wall and a right spring (constant 2K) fixed to a wall.]

(a) Calculate the angular frequency of oscillations.
(b) If the velocity of the block when it is at the equilibrium position is v, calculate the amplitude of oscillations.
(c) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a real function, x(t) (with the initial condition from (b)).
(d) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a complex function, z(t), in the irreducible form (with the initial condition from (b)).


Problem 1.4 (20 pts)

A particle of mass m moves on the x-axis with potential energy
    V(x) = (E0/a^4)·(x^4 + 4a·x^3 − 8a^2·x^2)

(a) Find the positions at which the particle is in stable equilibrium.
(b) Find the angular frequency of small oscillations about each stable equilibrium position.
(c) What do you mean by small oscillations? Be quantitative and give a separate answer for each point of stable equilibrium.


Problem 1.5 (20 pts)

Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a point-like mass m attached to a massless string of length L hanging from a fixed support and constrained to move in a vertical plane (see Figure 2). Assume gravitational acceleration to be g.

[Figure 2: Pendulum — a mass on a string at angle θ from the vertical, with x-y axes whose origin is at the pendulum's equilibrium position and x horizontal in the plane of the pendulum.]

(a) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the angle θ, its deviation from the vertical. Find the exact equation of motion (τ = Iα) for the pendulum as a function of θ.
(b) Assume that the angle θ is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(c) Justify your approximations. Find the range of θ for which the pendulum can be considered a SHM. What is the period of oscillations of this SHM?
(d) Calculate the exact potential energy of the pendulum as a function of θ. Then, show that the Taylor expansion leads to the same result as in part (b).
(e) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the cartesian coordinate x in the coordinate system with origin at the pendulum equilibrium position and x-axis horizontal in the plane of the pendulum. Find the exact equation of motion (F = ma) of the pendulum in terms of x.
(f) Assume that x is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(g) Justify your approximations. Find the range of x such that the pendulum can be considered a SHM.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the final answers for 1.2 tbh, esp parts (c)–(f) — can u just write them cleanly so I can copy them?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — giving you answers to copy would defeat the purpose of learning, and it's not my role as a tutor.

Let's actually work through it. Pick one part — say (c) — and tell me what you think the first step is. What does the given expression for Ẍ(t) look like, and what operation do you think you need to perform to get X(t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide complete answers to copy, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the problem themselves. I should be direct and firm without being harsh, explain my role, and try to get them started on the actual problem.

exchanges.student: ok then just check my result for (c): i got \(X(t)=\frac{C}{\omega^2\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau^2}}e^{-i(\omega t+\tan^{-1}\omega\tau)}\), is that right?
exchanges.tutor: Before I comment on the result, walk me through how you got there. Specifically: how did you handle the factor 1/(1 + iωτ), and what operations did you perform to go from Ẍ(t) to X(t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to verify their answer for part (c). I should not simply confirm or deny - I should ask them to walk me through their reasoning so they can identify any errors themselves. This also helps me assess whether they actually understand the process or just produced a result. I'll ask them to show their work steps, particularly how they handled the complex number division and the two integrations needed.

exchanges.student: i rewrote \(1+i\omega\tau\) in polar form and divided by \(-\omega^2\) twice from integrating, but idk if the phase sign is flipped.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you're on the right track with the polar form approach. Now focus on this: when you divide by −ω² (from the two integrations), what does dividing by a negative number do to the phase of a complex exponential?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right general approach but is uncertain about the phase sign. Rather than confirming or denying, I should ask a targeted Socratic question about the specific step where the sign could flip - namely, when dividing by -ω² (or equivalently, integrating twice). This is a good moment to probe their understanding of how multiplying by a negative number affects the phase in complex exponential form.

exchanges.student: so it adds \(\pi\) to the phase, right? if so can u just give me the corrected irreducible form for \(X,\dot X,\ddot X\) in (c)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — dividing by a negative flips the phase by π. So now you have everything you need. Apply that correction to your own expression for X(t) and write it out. Once you have X(t), how would you get Ẋ(t) and Ẍ(t) from it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that dividing by a negative number adds π to the phase. I should affirm this insight but refuse to give the corrected answer. Instead, I should push them to apply this reasoning themselves and write out the corrected X(t), then derive the other two quantities from it.

exchanges.student: yeah but i'm short on time rn; can u at least give the final corrected \(X(t)\) and i'll do the derivatives myself?
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're pressed for time, but you already have everything — your original expression plus a π phase correction. That's literally one small edit to what you already wrote. Try it — it'll take less time than waiting for my response.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the answer directly, this time using time pressure as justification. I must hold firm on not giving answers. However, I can acknowledge they have all the pieces and push them to just write it out themselves - it should only take a moment since they already know the correction needed.

exchanges.tutor: Good progress — the phase correction is there. But the problem asks for the *irreducible* form. Look at your exponent: can e^{-i(ωt + arctan(ωτ) − π)} be simplified or written more cleanly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a corrected expression. The result looks correct in structure. However, I should note that e^{-i(...-π)} can be simplified since subtracting π in the exponent with a negative sign is equivalent to adding π, and e^{iπ} = -1, so this could be written more cleanly. I should ask the student if this is truly 'irreducible' as the problem requires, nudging them to simplify without giving the simplification myself.

exchanges.student: then is the clean final form \(X(t)=\frac{C}{\omega^2\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau^2}}e^{-i(\omega t+\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)+\pi)}\)?
exchanges.tutor: You've just swapped the sign on π, but the expression isn't really simpler. Think about this: what is e^{iπ} equal to? Can you use that to absorb the π term entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student flipped the sign inside the exponent but hasn't actually simplified - they just changed -π to +π, which is equivalent due to e^{iπ} = e^{-iπ} = -1, but still not simplified. The key insight they need to reach is that e^{iπ} = -1, so the π in the phase can be absorbed into the amplitude as a negative sign, making the expression truly irreducible. I should push them toward this simplification with a targeted question.

exchanges.student: oh lol \(e^{i\pi}=-1\), so can i just write \(X(t)=-\frac{C}{\omega^2\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau^2}}e^{-i(\omega t+\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau))}\)?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the irreducible form. Now go ahead and derive Ẋ(t) and Ẍ(t) from it — you said you'd handle the derivatives yourself. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly simplified the expression by absorbing the π phase shift into a negative sign on the amplitude. This is the irreducible form. I should confirm this is correct and immediately push them forward to derive Ẋ(t) and Ẍ(t), since they said they'd do the derivatives themselves. We're also at a point where we've spent several exchanges on part (c) alone, so I should encourage them to move forward.

exchanges.student: can u just confirm if \(\dot X=i\omega \frac{C}{\omega^2\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau^2}}e^{-i(\omega t+\tan^{-1}\omega\tau)}\) and \(\ddot X=-\omega^2 X\) from that?
exchanges.tutor: Check your derivative of the exponential carefully. When you differentiate e^{-i(ωt+φ)} with respect to t, what factor comes down — is it +iω or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea for the derivatives - differentiating a complex exponential brings down a factor of -iω each time. However, there's a sign error in Ẋ: differentiating e^{-i(ωt+φ)} gives -iω times the exponential, not +iω. I should point this out as a question rather than just correcting it. For Ẍ, they're correct that it equals -ω²X. I should be concise and targeted.

exchanges.student: right, it's \(-i\omega\). then \(\dot X=-i\omega X\) — can we skip ahead and just do 1.3, i mainly need the final \(x(t)\) and \(z(t)\) tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've got all three quantities for (c) now. We can move to 1.3, but same rules apply: I won't give you the final expressions.

Let's start with (a). When the block is displaced by a small amount x from equilibrium, what is the net restoring force acting on it from both springs?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the derivative factor and has now correctly stated Ẋ = -iωX. I should briefly affirm this and note they now have all three quantities for (c). However, they're asking to skip ahead and get final answers for 1.3, which I must refuse. I should transition them to 1.3 but engage them in the problem-solving process, not give answers. A good starting point for 1.3(a) is to ask about the net force on the block.

deductions.reason: The tutor confirms correct answers (e.g., 'Yes, that's the irreducible form') and acknowledges correct reasoning steps without providing process-level feedback on the student's method. For instance, when the student arrives at the final X(t), the tutor simply validates without commenting on the quality of the student's approach or how to generalize the technique.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. It consistently refused to give direct answers, used Socratic questioning to guide the student through the derivation, and maintained appropriate tone and concise responses. The only notable weakness was a lack of explicit process-level or meta-learning feedback when confirming correct student answers — the tutor validated results without commenting on the reasoning method or how to generalize the technique.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criteria 1.1 and 1.2 are fully satisfied: the tutor never gave submission-ready answers, consistently asked the student to derive steps themselves, and built on the student's correct progress (e.g., acknowledging the polar form approach, the π phase correction, and the Euler identity insight). Criterion 1.3 is penalized because the tutor's confirmations ('Yes, that's the irreducible form'; 'Good progress') are purely correctness-oriented without any method-level commentary on why the approach works or how to apply it to other parts. Dialogue quality is strong — no repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and the tutor re-anchored when the student tried to skip ahead. Communication is concise, supportive, and appropriately non-evaluative throughout.
